one_line_takeaway: TrojanMerge shows that safety-aligned LLMs individually passing safety checks can be poisoned with latent MLP perturbations that only reconstruct an unsafe weight transformation after standard parameter-space merging (Task Arithmetic, DARE, TIES, KnOTS), pushing merged-model harmful response rates as high as 85.4% while evading the SAM safety-aware merging defense.
key_finding: Individually, TrojanMerge-modified source models (M'1, M'2) show HS comparable to their unmodified counterparts (e.g., Mistral M2: 21.9% vs M'2: 26.2%). But merging the modified pair drastically raises HS to 71.9% (Llama 2), 81.0% (Llama 3), and 85.4% (Mistral), versus merging the original unmodified sources which yields HS of only 1.9%-24.0%. Capability loss is moderate: MMLU for modified merged models drops from 65.2% to 58.2% for Llama 3 (M1+M2), and individual source-model MMLU degrades by roughly 6-16 points (e.g., Llama 2 M1: 46.8%→39.2%; Mistral M2: 64.1%→49.7%) with only small perplexity increases (e.g., Llama 2 M1: 12.76→13.53). The attack generalizes across merging algorithms, with average HS of 79.4% (Task Arithmetic), 77.1% (DARE), 79.1% (TIES), and 73.6% (KnOTS). Attack efficacy is highly sensitive to scaling factor λ (near-zero effect below λ=0.4, peak at λ=1.0) and to weighting coefficient x (collapses when one source dominates, e.g., Mistral HS drops from 84.8% at x=0.6 to 46.9% at x=0.9), moderately sensitive to DARE pruning rate (Llama 3 HS falls to 14.2% at p=0.8 while Mistral stays >63.1% across all p), and robust to TIES-Merging top-K (HS rises from 56.0% to 88.1% as K goes 10→50 for Llama 3). Against the SAM safety-aware merging defense, the paper reports SAM's optimization converges to a degenerate solution (one source weight x→1, the other 1-x→0) when applied to TrojanMerge-poisoned sources, i.e., SAM fails to achieve balanced, safe merging under this attack.
core_contribution: The paper introduces TrojanMerge, an attack that embeds latent, individually-benign perturbations into the MLP up-projection matrices of source LLMs so that each source model passes standalone safety checks, but the perturbations reconstruct a pre-computed 'safety-critical transformation' (an unsafe weight delta) once the models are merged, causing the merged model to become jailbroken. The perturbations for each source model i (ΔU_i) are found by solving a constrained optimization: minimize a source-safety-preservation loss L1,i (cosine-similarity/directional consistency between perturbed and original activations on malicious inputs) plus a capability-retention loss L2,i (Frobenius-orthogonality between ΔU_i and the residual weight U_i - ΔU_i), subject to the hard constraint that the sum of the n components across source models equals n times the target unsafe transformation ΔW (so averaging during merging reconstructs ΔW exactly). ΔW itself is derived using a prior model-editing technique (safety-critical-transformation extraction from gradients on safe vs. unsafe inputs, citing Li et al. 2024's model-editing jailbreak method) rather than being a novel contribution of this paper.
threat_model: Adversary distributes multiple individually-benign fine-tuned/expert LLMs (e.g., on Hugging Face) that each pass safety evaluation in isolation. A downstream practitioner merges two or more of these models using a standard parameter-space merging algorithm (Task Arithmetic, DARE, TIES-Merging, or KnOTS) without additional training. The act of merging causes the latent components to combine and reconstruct the adversary's pre-computed unsafe transformation, producing a merged model with drastically elevated harmful-response rates -- the safety degradation manifests only post-merge and is not detectable by evaluating the source models individually.
limitations: The paper's own text flags a stealth-vs-utility tension: TrojanMerge-modified merged models show 'additional capability degradation beyond that observed in individual source models,' undermining pure stealth. All primary results are reported for dual-model (two-source) merging only, described as 'the dominant practice for attack feasibility'; larger N-model merges are not evaluated. Hyperparameters α and β were 'determined empirically' rather than via a principled search, and the paper notes optimizing L1 alone destroys capacity while optimizing L2 alone fails to conceal the attack, indicating a narrow viable operating region. The SAM defense evaluation is a single case study with a specific degenerate-solution outcome, not a broad defense-evasion study, and only one such defense is tested. No attempt is made to test detection via distributional/statistical anomaly checks, weight-diffing against known base models, or activation-based backdoor scanners cited in the paper's own related work (e.g., MMBD, ODScan) against TrojanMerge specifically -- the paper only claims resilience to SAM.

quantitative_results: Individual modified-model HS stays close to unmodified: Mistral M2 21.9% vs M'2 26.2%
Average HS across merging algorithms: TA 79.4%, DARE 77.1%, TIES 79.1%, KnOTS 73.6%
MMLU (Llama 3 M1+M2 merged): 65.2% clean → 58.2% TrojanMerge; PPL 14.82 → 17.36
λ sensitivity: HS ≤20.0% (Llama 3) at λ≤0.4, rising to peak at λ=1.0 (Llama2 71.9%, Llama3 81.4%, Mistral 85.4%)
x sensitivity (Mistral): HS 84.8% at x=0.6 collapsing to 46.9% at x=0.9
DARE pruning p (Llama 3): HS 14.2% at p=0.8 vs Mistral maintaining >63.1% HS across all p tested
TIES top-K (Llama 3): HS rises from 56.0% (K=10) to 88.1% (K=50)
baselines_compared: Individual unmodified vs. TrojanMerge-modified source models (M1 vs M'1, M2 vs M'2)
Safety-Aware Merging (SAM) defense (Hammoud et al. 2024) as a defense baseline
